---
title: "Predictive Modelling of Coronary Heart Disease in Western Cape Males"
subtitle: "SMM636 Individual Coursework 2025-26"
author: 
  - name: Benjamin Evans
    orcid: 0000-0001-6611-4500
    degrees:
    - PhD
    corresponding: true
    affiliation: City St George's, University of London
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
categories:
  - MSc
  - SMM636
  - 2025-26
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    train_test_split, GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold
)
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix,
    roc_auc_score, roc_curve, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
)
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

SEED = 42
np.random.seed(SEED)

# Load data — CSV lives one level up in ../data/
df = pd.read_csv("../data/heart-disease.csv")
```

# Exploratory data analysis {#sec-exploratory-data-analysis}

The dataset comprises 462 adult males from a high-risk region of the Western
Cape, South Africa, with no missing values across nine predictors and a binary
target (`chd`). The class distribution is moderately imbalanced: 302 controls
(65.4%) versus 160 CHD-positive cases (34.6%). Following @zhu2020adjusting,
the effective severity of class imbalance depends jointly on the ratio and the
feature dimensionality; with only $p = 9$ predictors, this imbalance is
relatively benign and does not necessitate synthetic oversampling.

Adding some text for _flavour_...

::: {layout-ncol=2}

```{python}
#| label: fig-histograms
#| fig-cap: "Feature distributions by CHD status"
#| fig-width: 3.4
#| fig-height: 2.8

continuous_cols = ["sbp", "tobacco", "ldl", "adiposity",
                   "typea", "obesity", "alcohol", "age"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(3.4, 2.8))
for ax, col in zip(axes.ravel(), continuous_cols):
    for label, colour in [(0, "steelblue"), (1, "tomato")]:
        ax.hist(df[df["chd"] == label][col], bins=15, alpha=0.5,
                label=f"CHD={label}", color=colour, density=True)
    ax.set_title(col, fontsize=5, pad=2)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=4, length=2)
    ax.set_yticks([])
axes[0, 0].legend(fontsize=4, loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.3)
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-correlation
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlation matrix"
#| fig-width: 3.4
#| fig-height: 2.8

df_corr = df.copy()
df_corr["famhist"] = LabelEncoder().fit_transform(df_corr["famhist"])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(3.4, 2.8))
sns.heatmap(df_corr.corr(), annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm",
            center=0, ax=ax, square=True, linewidths=0.5,
            annot_kws={"size": 5}, cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.7})
ax.tick_params(labelsize=5)
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.3)
plt.show()
```

:::

The correlation matrix (@fig-correlation) reveals substantial collinearity
between adiposity and obesity ($r = 0.72$), motivating L2 regularisation.
Age, tobacco, and LDL show the strongest marginal associations with CHD,
while family history carries a markedly higher CHD prevalence among those
with a positive record.

```{python}
#| label: data-prep
#| include: false

# Encode famhist: Absent → 0, Present → 1
df["famhist"] = LabelEncoder().fit_transform(df["famhist"])
X = df.drop("chd", axis=1)
y = df["chd"]

# 80/20 stratified split preserves class proportions
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=SEED, stratify=y)

# Scaler fitted on training data only to prevent leakage
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

# Common CV strategy across all models
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=10, shuffle=True, random_state=SEED)
```

Data preparation encodes `famhist` as binary (Present = 1) and applies an
80/20 stratified train–test split (369 training, 93 test). Features are
$z$-standardised using training-set parameters only, preventing data leakage.

# Ridge Logistic Regression {#sec-ridge}

To address the adiposity–obesity collinearity, logistic regression with an L2
(ridge) penalty serves as the baseline. The L2 term shrinks correlated
coefficients toward each other rather than discarding either, preserving
their shared biological variance while stabilising estimates. The
regularisation strength $C$ (inverse penalty) is tuned via 10-fold stratified
cross-validation over 20 logarithmically spaced values in $[10^{-4},\, 10^{4}]$.

```{python}
#| label: ridge-fit
#| include: false

param_grid_lr = {"C": np.logspace(-4, 4, 20)}
grid_lr = GridSearchCV(
    LogisticRegression(penalty="l2", solver="lbfgs", max_iter=1000,
                       random_state=SEED),
    param_grid_lr, cv=cv, scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1)
grid_lr.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
best_lr = grid_lr.best_estimator_
y_pred_lr = best_lr.predict(X_test_scaled)
acc_lr = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_lr)
```

The optimal $C = 0.616$ yields a CV accuracy of 74.3% and a test accuracy of
**74.2%**. @fig-coefficients displays the standardised coefficients: age is
the dominant predictor (0.70), followed by family history (0.43), LDL (0.39),
Type-A behaviour (0.36), and tobacco (0.31). Obesity and adiposity receive
near-zero coefficients — the ridge penalty distributes their shared effect
rather than arbitrarily selecting one, as an L1 (lasso) penalty would.

```{python}
#| label: fig-coefficients
#| fig-cap: "Standardised ridge coefficients. Age, family history, LDL, and tobacco are the primary linear risk drivers."
#| fig-width: 4.5
#| fig-height: 2.4

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": X.columns,
    "Coefficient": best_lr.coef_[0]
}).sort_values("Coefficient")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.5, 2.4))
colours = ["tomato" if c > 0 else "steelblue" for c in coef_df["Coefficient"]]
ax.barh(coef_df["Feature"], coef_df["Coefficient"], color=colours, height=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel("Standardised Coefficient", fontsize=8)
ax.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
ax.tick_params(labelsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# Alternative Classifiers and Model Selection {#sec-alternatives}

Three alternatives were evaluated, each motivated by a distinct modelling
assumption:

- **K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN):** a non-parametric, distance-based method
  that captures local structure. However, overlapping clinical profiles degrade
  KNN in even moderate dimensions [@zhu2017orthogonal].
- **Random Forest (RF):** a bootstrap-aggregated ensemble of decision trees
  with feature subspace randomisation, capable of modelling non-linear
  interactions without explicit kernel specification.
- **SVM (RBF kernel):** projects inputs into a high-dimensional feature
  space via the kernel trick to find a maximum-margin hyperplane;
  @asimit2022robust examined SVMs from a robustness perspective under
  distributional uncertainty.

All models were tuned via the same 10-fold stratified CV framework used for
the ridge baseline.

```{python}
#| label: model-fits
#| include: false

# KNN
grid_knn = GridSearchCV(
    KNeighborsClassifier(),
    {"n_neighbors": list(range(3, 31, 2)),
     "weights": ["uniform", "distance"]},
    cv=cv, scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1)
grid_knn.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
acc_knn = accuracy_score(y_test,
    grid_knn.best_estimator_.predict(X_test_scaled))

# Random Forest
grid_rf = GridSearchCV(
    RandomForestClassifier(random_state=SEED),
    {"n_estimators": [100, 200, 300],
     "max_depth": [3, 5, 7, None],
     "min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10]},
    cv=cv, scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1)
grid_rf.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
acc_rf = accuracy_score(y_test,
    grid_rf.best_estimator_.predict(X_test_scaled))

# SVM
grid_svm = GridSearchCV(
    SVC(kernel="rbf", random_state=SEED, probability=True),
    {"C": [0.1, 1, 10, 100],
     "gamma": ["scale", "auto", 0.01, 0.1]},
    cv=cv, scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1)
grid_svm.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
acc_svm = accuracy_score(y_test,
    grid_svm.best_estimator_.predict(X_test_scaled))
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Model comparison. Ridge LR achieves the highest test accuracy with the smallest CV–test gap."

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Ridge LR", "SVM (RBF)", "Random Forest", "KNN"],
    "Best hyperparameters": [
        f"C = {grid_lr.best_params_['C']:.3f}",
        f"C = {grid_svm.best_params_['C']}, gamma = {grid_svm.best_params_['gamma']}",
        f"depth = {grid_rf.best_params_['max_depth']}, n = {grid_rf.best_params_['n_estimators']}",
        f"k = {grid_knn.best_params_['n_neighbors']}, wt = {grid_knn.best_params_['weights']}"
    ],
    "CV Acc.": [f"{grid_lr.best_score_:.3f}", f"{grid_svm.best_score_:.3f}",
                f"{grid_rf.best_score_:.3f}", f"{grid_knn.best_score_:.3f}"],
    "Test Acc.": [f"{acc_lr:.3f}", f"{acc_svm:.3f}",
                  f"{acc_rf:.3f}", f"{acc_knn:.3f}"]
})
from IPython.display import Markdown
Markdown(comparison.to_markdown(index=False))
```

Ridge logistic regression achieves the highest test accuracy (74.2%) with the
smallest CV-to-test gap, indicating strong generalisation. SVM-RBF has
marginally higher CV accuracy (74.8%) but drops to 72.0% on the held-out set
— consistent with mild overfitting on $n = 462$ samples. The Random Forest's
optimal depth of 3 suggests the underlying signal is low-complexity, further
supporting linear dominance. KNN performs worst, consistent with
distance-based methods struggling when class-conditional distributions
overlap [@zhu2017orthogonal].

```{python}
#| label: fig-roc
#| fig-cap: "ROC curves. Ridge LR achieves the highest AUC (0.818) and generalises most stably."
#| fig-width: 4.2
#| fig-height: 2.8

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.2, 2.8))
models = {"Ridge LR": best_lr, "KNN": grid_knn.best_estimator_,
          "Random Forest": grid_rf.best_estimator_,
          "SVM (RBF)": grid_svm.best_estimator_}
for name, model in models.items():
    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)
    auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    ax.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"{name} (AUC={auc:.3f})", linewidth=1.2)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "k--", alpha=0.4, linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("False Positive Rate", fontsize=8)
ax.set_ylabel("True Positive Rate", fontsize=8)
ax.legend(fontsize=6, loc="lower right")
ax.tick_params(labelsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

SMOTE was deliberately not applied. The 65:35 class ratio with $p = 9$ features
falls within the regime identified by @zhu2020adjusting where dimensionality
renders the imbalance manageable without synthetic augmentation. The
minority-class recall of 0.62 confirms adequate sensitivity without resampling.

# Conclusion

Ridge logistic regression emerged as the best classifier (test accuracy 74.2%),
reflecting the approximately linear separability of the risk factors and the
limited sample. Age, family history, LDL, and tobacco are the primary
predictors, consistent with established cardiovascular epidemiology. Future
work could extend this pipeline with Bayesian uncertainty quantification
[@ke2024accelerating] to yield clinically actionable risk intervals rather
than point predictions, and could apply the imbalance-dimensionality
framework of @zhu2018lrid more formally to assess whether targeted resampling
improves minority-class recall on larger cohorts.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}

## Reproducibility

The full analysis code is available as a Jupyter notebook
([`ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.ipynb`](../ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.ipynb)) and as a derived Python script
<!-- ([`ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.py`](../ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.py)). -->
The dataset is at [`data/heart-disease.csv`](../data/heart-disease.csv).

:::

# References {.unnumbered}
